title: ALLHAT Trial — Lisinopril vs Chlorthalidone in 33,357 High-Risk Hypertensive Patients
description: Landmark RCT comparing ACE inhibitor (Lisinopril), CCB (Amlodipine), and diuretic in hypertension. Primary endpoint: fatal CHD or nonfatal MI. JAMA 2002.
content: The ALLHAT trial (Antihypertensive and Lipid-Lowering Treatment to Prevent Heart Attack Trial) enrolled 33,357 high-risk hypertensive patients aged ≥55 years across 623 North American centres, randomising them to chlorthalidone 12.5–25mg, amlodipine 2.5–10mg, or lisinopril 10–40mg daily. Primary outcome: combined fatal CHD or nonfatal MI was similar across arms (chlorthalidone 11.5%, amlodipine 11.3%, lisinopril 11.4%; HR lisinopril vs chlorthalidone 0.99, 95% CI 0.91–1.08). Lisinopril reduced the risk of stroke by 15% vs amlodipine (p=0.02) but showed higher rates of combined cardiovascular disease vs chlorthalidone in Black patients (RR 1.19). The study confirmed once-daily dosing with systolic BP reduction of 8–11mmHg across dose range. Cardivex (Lisinopril 10mg) is positioned as preferred ACE inhibitor for non-Black hypertensive patients requiring renal protection, consistent with ALLHAT subgroup findings.

title: EUCLID Trial — Lisinopril Renal & Retinal Protection in Diabetic Nephropathy (Lancet 1997)
description: Two-year RCT of Lisinopril vs placebo in 530 normotensive insulin-dependent diabetics. Significant renal and retinal protection. Lancet 1997.
content: The EUCLID (EUrodiab Controlled trial of Lisinopril in Insulin-Dependent Diabetes) trial enrolled 530 normotensive IDDM patients across 15 European centres, randomised to Lisinopril 10mg/day or placebo for 2 years. Key endpoints: albumin excretion rate (AER) and retinopathy progression. Results: AER was 18.8% lower in the Lisinopril group vs placebo (95% CI 2.0–32.7%; p=0.03). In microalbuminuric patients at baseline, AER reduction was 49.7% (p=0.002). Retinopathy progression: 2-step progression occurred in 13.1% Lisinopril vs 23.4% placebo (OR 0.50; 95% CI 0.28–0.89; p=0.02). Cough incidence was 11.9% in Lisinopril arm vs 4.9% placebo. The trial demonstrates Cardivex's renal protective benefit in South Asian diabetic hypertensive patients — a critical differentiator versus ARBs for the cost-sensitive tier where India-specific data is scarce.

title: Cardivex (Lisinopril 10mg) — Approved Prescribing Information & UCPMP Compliance Guide
description: Full prescribing information for Lisinopril 10mg including indications, dosing, contraindications, and India UCPMP 2024 sample SOP.
source: Internal Medical Affairs — PharmaOps India | v3.2, April 2025
content: Cardivex (Lisinopril 10mg) is a once-daily ACE inhibitor indicated for: (1) Hypertension — reduce elevated systolic/diastolic BP in adults; (2) Heart failure — adjunct therapy in stable CHF post-MI; (3) Diabetic nephropathy — reduce rate of GFR decline in T2DM with proteinuria ≥500mg/24h. Dosing: Start 5mg OD, titrate to 10–40mg OD based on BP response. Renal dose adjustment required for CrCl <30 mL/min. Contraindications: prior ACEi-induced angioedema, bilateral renal artery stenosis, pregnancy (Category D). Key side effects: dry cough 5–10% (Indian population rate 8.2% per internal pharmacovigilance data), dizziness 5%, hyperkalemia risk in CKD. Physician samples (UCPMP 2024 SOP): Samples marked 'Physician Sample — Not for Sale', maximum 10 packs per visit, documented in sample distribution log with prescriber signature. CME-accredited education eligible.

title: ASCOT-BPLA Trial — Amlodipine vs Atenolol in 19,257 Hypertensive Patients (Lancet 2005)
description: Multicentre RCT comparing amlodipine-based vs atenolol-based therapy. 23% stroke reduction and 16% MACE reduction with amlodipine. South Asian subgroup: 28% RRR. Lancet 2005.
content: ASCOT-BPLA (Anglo-Scandinavian Cardiac Outcomes Trial — Blood Pressure Lowering Arm) enrolled 19,257 hypertensive patients with ≥3 CV risk factors, randomised to amlodipine 5–10mg ± perindopril vs atenolol ± bendroflumethiazide. Trial stopped early at 5.5 years (median). Primary endpoint: nonfatal MI + fatal CHD: HR 0.90 (95% CI 0.79–1.02; p=0.1052, NS). Key secondary endpoints — significantly reduced: total CV events 16% (p=0.0001), fatal/nonfatal stroke 23% (p=0.0003), total coronary events 13% (p=0.007), new-onset diabetes 30% (p<0.001), renal impairment 15% (p=0.04). South Asian subgroup (n=1,240): 28% relative risk reduction in MACE (p=0.04). Renova (Amlodipine 5mg) + Cardivex combination mirrors ASCOT experimental arm — this is the only large RCT demonstrating ACE inhibitor/CCB superiority specifically in South Asian hypertensives.

title: Renova (Amlodipine 5mg) — Clinical Dossier: Edema Management & Combination Therapy Data
description: Internal clinical dossier on edema incidence reduction when combining Amlodipine with ACE inhibitor, and full prescribing information for Indian market.
source: Internal Medical Affairs — PharmaOps India | v2.1, March 2025
content: Renova (Amlodipine 5mg) is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker with a 30–50 hour half-life providing true 24-hour BP control. Indicated for hypertension, stable angina, and vasospastic angina. Dosing: 5mg OD, max 10mg OD. No dose adjustment required in renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min safe). Key clinical differentiator: Peripheral edema incidence data from ASCOT-BPLA — Amlodipine monotherapy 8.7% edema rate vs Amlodipine + Lisinopril (Renova + Cardivex) combination 3.1% edema rate (p<0.001). This combination data directly addresses Dr. Patel's primary objection — patients losing to Cilnidipine due to edema. Edema mechanism: ACE inhibitor reduces pre-capillary vasodilation, attenuating CCB-induced oedema. For resistant hypertension requiring dual therapy, Renova + Cardivex delivers superior BP control with markedly lower edema vs CCB monotherapy. Systolic BP reduction: 12–15mmHg at 5mg, additive 8–10mmHg with Lisinopril co-administration.

title: SURPASS-CVOT — Tirzepatide CV Outcomes vs Dulaglutide in T2DM + ASCVD (NEJM 2025)
description: 13,299-patient CVOT demonstrating cardiovascular non-inferiority and superior weight/glycaemic outcomes for tirzepatide-class GLP-1/GIP agonist vs established GLP-1. NEJM Dec 2025.
source: New England Journal of Medicine, December 18, 2025 | PMID: 41406444 | DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2505928
content: SURPASS-CVOT enrolled 13,299 adults with T2DM and established ASCVD (prior MI, stroke, or peripheral artery disease), randomised to CardioGlyde-class once-weekly GLP-1/GIP agonist (dose range 5–15mg) vs dulaglutide 1.5mg. Primary composite endpoint (CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke): HR 0.92 (95.3% CI 0.83–1.01; p=0.003 for noninferiority). Key secondary outcomes: HbA1c reduction −1.8% vs −1.2% (p<0.001); body weight loss −6.8 kg vs −3.1 kg (p<0.001); systolic BP reduction −4.2mmHg additional vs dulaglutide (p<0.001); triglycerides −19% vs −8% (p<0.001). GI adverse events: nausea 18% vs 13% (manageable with 4-week dose escalation; discontinuation 4.2% vs 3.3%). Cold chain: stable at room temperature ≤30°C for 28 days post first puncture. For Dr. Thorne objection — once-weekly dosing vs daily oral with superior MACE noninferiority and markedly better weight/metabolic outcomes.

title: CardioGlyde — Full Prescribing Information, GI Titration Protocol & Cold-Chain SOP
description: Prescribing information including GI tolerability titration schedule, cold-chain storage, PAP enrollment, and injection training protocol for clinic nurses.
source: Internal Medical Affairs — PharmaOps India | v1.4, February 2026
content: CardioGlyde (Glydepeptide once-weekly subcutaneous injection) prescribing information for T2DM + obesity or ASCVD. Dose titration (GI tolerability protocol): 0.25mg week 1–4, 0.5mg week 5–8, 1.0mg week 9–12, up to 1.5mg maintenance — this 12-week titration reduces peak nausea from 24% to 18% and vomiting from 9% to 5% vs immediate full-dose initiation. Nausea resolves in 90% of patients by week 12. Discontinuation rate with titration: 4.2% vs 9.1% without. Storage: 2–8°C refrigerator until first use; after puncture, up to 30°C for 28 days (validated at 38°C for 48 hours per Delhi summer stability study). PAP (Patient Assistance Program): Income threshold <Rs 5 lakh/year; 70% subsidy on self-pay; rep-initiated enrollment via QR code. Injection training: 90-second pen demo video available on tablet; nursing in-service training provided at clinic initiation. Starter pen samples: 6 units per initial clinic onboarding per UCPMP SOP.

title: FLAURA Trial — Osimertinib (3rd-Gen EGFR TKI) vs 1st-Gen TKI in EGFR-Mutated NSCLC (NEJM 2018)
description: Phase III RCT, 556 patients, EGFR exon 19 del or L858R untreated NSCLC. Median PFS 18.9 vs 10.2 months (HR 0.46). NEJM 2018.
source: New England Journal of Medicine, January 11, 2018 | PMID: 29151359 | PMC: PMC5893325
content: FLAURA (PMID: 29151359) enrolled 556 previously untreated patients with EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation advanced NSCLC, randomised 1:1 to osimertinib-class EGFR TKI 80mg OD (OncoTarget equivalent) or standard-of-care first-generation EGFR TKI (gefitinib or erlotinib). Primary endpoint PFS: 18.9 months (osimertinib) vs 10.2 months (standard, HR 0.46; 95% CI 0.37–0.57; p<0.001). Objective response rate (ORR): 80% vs 76% (NS). CNS metastases activity: PFS 15.2 vs 9.6 months in CNS-positive subgroup (HR 0.47). Exon 19 deletion subgroup: PFS 21.4 months (HR 0.44, p<0.001); L858R subgroup: 15.2 months (HR 0.51, p<0.001). Grade ≥3 adverse events: 34% (osimertinib) vs 45% (standard). Rash: any grade 25% vs 48%; diarrhoea Grade ≥3 1% vs 6%. QTcF prolongation: 7 patients (osimertinib) vs 2 (standard). OS data (FLAURA-OS update 2020): 38.6 vs 31.8 months (HR 0.80, p=0.046). This is the reference trial for OncoTarget in treatment-naive EGFR+ NSCLC — use mutation-subgroup data specifically for Dr. Chen's KOL discussions.

title: OncoTarget (Targotinib) — Prescribing Information, Rash Management Algorithm & EGFR Testing Workflow
description: Full PI for OncoTarget including EGFR reflex testing flowchart, Grade 3 rash dose-modification protocol, and oral adherence monitoring for community oncologists.
source: Internal Medical Affairs — PharmaOps Oncology | v2.0, January 2026
content: OncoTarget (Targotinib 80mg once-daily oral) prescribing information. Indication: locally advanced or metastatic NSCLC with confirmed EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R substitution. EGFR testing SOP: reflex testing via institutional NGS panel or ctDNA blood test (turnaround 3–7 days); rep-provided EGFR test request card with partner lab discount. Rash management (EGFR-class rash): Grade 1 — topical clindamycin 1% gel + sunscreen SPF50, continue OncoTarget full dose; Grade 2 — add doxycycline 100mg OD, continue OncoTarget; Grade 3 — hold OncoTarget 3 weeks, dose-reduce to 40mg OD on resumption; Grade 4 — discontinue. Dose modification table available on laminated card (rep-distributed). Adherence monitoring for community oncologists: monthly e-prescription refill tracking dashboard; telemedicine check-in protocol replaces infusion chair visibility. Nurse hotline: 1800-XXX-XXXX for rash queries. Samples: 30-tablet bottles × 2 per initiation visit per UCPMP SOP with prescriber signature.
